text: The surgeon begins the operation by making an incision in the peritoneum, the thin lining of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to work laterally towards the midline, revealing the hernia as a visible hole in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon dissects tissues from the peritoneal lining, revealing part of the lining that enters the hernia and forms the hernia sac.
The surgeon visually identifies the peritoneal lining forming the hernia sac as it protrudes into the hernia defect within the abdominal wall.
The surgeon retracts the hernia contents, including visible adipose tissue, back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the pubis and Cooper's ligament, serving as the anatomical landmark for the medial point of dissection during the hernia repair procedure.
The hernia sac is visible with its contents fully reduced back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon places a piece of mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the area adequately to reinforce the abdominal wall.
The surgeon places a self-fixating mesh against the abdominal wall, which adheres without the need for stitches or tacks, resembling Velcro in its ability to stick securely.
The surgeon ensures the self-fixating mesh is properly positioned against the abdominal wall, covering the hernia defect without the need for stitches or tacks.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh, adding a protective layer to prevent the mesh from adhering to the intestines.
The surgeon uses the hernia sac and surrounding fat to reinforce the closure, ensuring these tissues are positioned to prevent hernia recurrence.
The surgeon uses an absorbable suture that does not require knots to secure the surgical site.
The surgeon completes the operation by cutting the suture after it is run back on itself, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned against the abdominal wall.
